head_coach.name: Todd Bowles
head_coach.scheme_philosophy: Aggressive, multiple-front defense with heavy blitz rates and man coverage roots. Bowles also calls the defensive plays, functioning as de facto DC. Adaptable to personnel — has run both 3-4 and 4-3 schemes throughout career. On offense, has empowered McVay-tree coordinators to run high-tempo, pass-friendly systems.

offensive_coordinator.name: Zac Robinson
offensive_coordinator.scheme_tree: Sean McVay / Shanahan-Kubiak wide zone, run-pass marriage

defensive_coordinator.name: Todd Bowles (HC calls defense)
defensive_coordinator.scheme_tree: Rex Ryan-adjacent aggressive blitz, roots in secondary coaching; influenced by Bruce Arians, Wade Phillips, Andy Reid staffs

offense.scheme_family: McVay run-pass marriage / West Coast hybrid
offense.personnel_detail: Under Coen in 2024, Tampa ran ~78% 11 personnel early in the season. Robinson used 11 personnel predominantly in 2024 ATL but shifted to 38.2% 12 personnel in 2025 ATL. Expect flexible personnel usage in Tampa with a 11-personnel base but willingness to deploy 12 and even 13 sets depending on matchup.
offense.run_scheme: outside zone primary, with gap/power elements mixed in; Robinson's Falcons were No. 1 in outside-the-tackles run rate
offense.pass_concepts: run-pass marriage under center, high pre-snap motion rate, quick-game and play-action concepts, exploit leverage advantages through motion. Robinson emphasizes intent behind every play rather than just running plays to run plays.
offense.te_usage: Dual-role: Otton is an elite blocker (993 snaps in 2025) who also runs a full route tree. Used on chip-and-release, sift blocks, and can flex into routes. Robinson is known to employ TEs to move the chains and used heavy 12/13 personnel in ATL in 2025. TE receiving role is moderate-to-high but blocking demands are significant.
offense.rb_usage: Committee approach with Bucky Irving as lead back. Shanahan/McVay-tree offenses traditionally value receiving backs. Rachaad White was used as receiving/third-down back before entering free agency. Backs expected to be involved in pass game.

defense.alignment: Odd front base (3 down linemen, 4 LBs) but frequently morphs into 4-2 nickel, 3-3-5, 2-4-5, and 4-3 looks depending on opponent and down/distance. One-gap attacking scheme, NOT a two-gap 3-4.
defense.edge_rush_source: OLB stand-up primary (Yaya Diaby, Haason Reddick types), but Bowles moves rushers between hands-in-dirt and stand-up constantly. DL also generates interior pressure with Vita Vea and Calijah Kancey.
defense.coverage_base: Man-heavy with Cover 1 / Cover 0 as base tendency. Will mix in Cover 3 and zone looks for disguise. MOFC (middle of field closed) is predominant pre-snap look. Heavy use of press coverage with length at corner.
defense.blitz_tendency: heavy (38.9% blitz rate in 2024, 2nd highest in NFL). Triple A-gap pressure is a signature. Bowles sends exotic zone blitzes, overload blitzes, and fire blitzes from multiple positions including safeties and corners.
defense.lb_role: ILBs (Lavonte David, SirVocea Dennis) are run-and-coverage players who also blitz from A-gaps. OLBs (Diaby, Reddick, David Walker) are primary edge rushers who stand up on the line. OLBs in this scheme function as pure edge rushers.
defense.safety_usage: Both safeties play hybrid roles. Antoine Winfield Jr. is an All-Pro used interchangeably in the post, box, and slot. Tykee Smith plays strong safety as a box/hybrid defender — led all safeties in passes defensed (13) in 2025, posted 100+ tackles, and is used as a blitzer. Bowles created the 'moneybacker' role (safety-LB hybrid) in Arizona with Deone Bucannon and continues to use safeties this way.

te_receiving_boost.explanation: Robinson's McVay-tree offense does utilize TEs in the passing game — Robinson employed Kyle Pitts aggressively in ATL and shifted to 38.2% 12-personnel in 2025. Cade Otton ran a full route tree and finished with 59 catches in 2025 while also being a primary blocker. However, Tampa's TE role is dual-purpose: significant blocking demands (993 snaps, sift blocks, chip-and-release) alongside receiving. A pure receiving TE gets a moderate boost but must also block in this scheme. The move toward heavier personnel (12/13 groupings) increases TE targets but demands versatility.

lb_pass_rush_boost.explanation: Bowles' 3-4 base directly features OLBs as the primary edge rushers. Yaya Diaby (7.0 sacks, 16.3% pressure rate in 2025), Haason Reddick, and Shaquil Barrett before him were all listed as OLBs but function as pure edge rushers. Any LB prospect with elite pass rush traits — burst, bend, first step — would be deployed on the edge in Tampa's stand-up rush packages. The scheme is explicitly designed around OLBs rushing the passer. Larry Foote coaches the OLB room specifically as a pass-rush unit.

rb_dual_threat_boost.explanation: McVay-tree offenses historically value pass-catching backs. Robinson's scheme emphasizes run-pass marriage, where the RB is involved in both elements on any given play. Tampa used Rachaad White as a receiving/third-down back and Bucky Irving flashed receiving ability as a rookie. The offense distributes the ball widely and backs are part of the progression. However, Tampa has also shown a willingness to feature a power runner (Irving ran for 1,000 yards as a rookie in 2024), so pure running ability still carries significant value. Receiving RBs get a moderate but not dominant boost.

s_hybrid_boost.explanation: This is one of the NFL's most hybrid-friendly safety schemes. Bowles invented the 'moneybacker' role in Arizona with Deone Bucannon, and the tradition continues. Antoine Winfield Jr. is a prototype — box safety, slot corner, blitzer, and deep safety all in one. Tykee Smith moved from nickel corner to strong safety in 2025 and posted 100+ tackles, 13 PDs, 2 sacks, and 3+ takeaways. Bowles called Smith 'probably our best player.' Both safeties are deployed interchangeably in the post and box. Bowles runs formations with 3 safeties on the field. Any safety prospect with tackling, blitz ability, man coverage skill, and versatility gets a massive value boost for Tampa.

scheme_specific_draft_notes: Tampa Bay is elite at maximizing hybrid defenders. Bowles' 3-4 defense makes OLB pass rushers the most valuable defensive commodity — any LB with edge rush traits (burst, bend, power moves) should be valued at EDGE prices for TB. The safety position is equally critical: Bowles demands safeties who can play box, slot, blitz, and deep — the Winfield/Smith mold. Prospects like nickel/safety hybrids get a significant TB premium. On offense, new OC Zac Robinson brings a McVay-tree scheme emphasizing run-pass marriage, outside zone, and heavy pre-snap motion. Robinson showed willingness to use 12/13 personnel heavily (38.2% 12-personnel in ATL 2025), which increases TE value but demands blocking versatility — pure receiving TEs without blocking ability are less ideal. With Mike Evans potentially departing in free agency, the WR room (Egbuka, McMillan, Godwin) may need reinforcement, elevating WR value. The 15th overall pick likely targets either EDGE (Cashius Howell), LB (CJ Allen), or secondary help. TB's blitz-heavy scheme (38.9% blitz rate) means man-coverage corners with length and press ability are premium fits. The pass rush produced its lowest sack total in Bowles' seven seasons in 2025, making edge rusher the most pressing need.
